title: Leviticus 2
subtitle: The Grain Offering: The Firstfruits Belong to God

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — The Raw and Baked Grain Offerings

heb.paragraph: Originally any gift or tribute (Gen 4:3; 32:14). In Leviticus it narrows to the cereal offering. Signals the produce of human labour presented as tribute to the divine King.
The best-grade flour — sifted, premium quality. Only the finest tribute is fitting for God.
The representative portion burned on the altar as an ʾazkārāh (memorial/token portion). The rest belongs to Aaron and his sons.

refs.note: "I am the bread of life." The grain offering's bread symbolism points forward — Christ is the true bread from heaven, the firstfruits of a new humanity.
"Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed. Therefore let us keep the festival — not with the old leaven… but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth."
The grain offering always accompanies animal sacrifices in the wilderness — it is never offered alone as a burnt offering is. It functions as complement, not substitute.

notes.note: MacArthur: The grain offering acknowledges God as Lord of the harvest — every crop, every loaf, every meal ultimately derives from his provision. Presenting the first and finest is an act of covenant loyalty.

MacArthur: The five forms of grain offering (raw, oven-baked, griddle, pan, deep-fry) ensure that every method of food preparation can become an act of worship. No sphere of daily life lies outside the sacred.

Calvin: The grain offering teaches that our labour and its fruits belong to God. We are stewards, not owners — the field, the harvest, the bread on the table all derive from divine bounty and are rightfully returned in acknowledgement.

Calvin: The passage on grain offering demonstrates that God's commands are never arbitrary but expressions of his character applied to Israel's specific circumstances. What he requires, he also enables — the law reveals both the divine standard and the need for grace.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The term minḥāh shifts in meaning across the OT — from "gift/tribute" generically (Gen 4:3) to specifically "grain offering" in the priestly texts. Leviticus 2 establishes its technical cultic sense.

NET Note: Salt was the ancient covenant medium — covenants were ratified with a shared salt meal (cf. Ezra 4:14, "we eat the salt of the palace"). The "salt of the covenant" in every grain offering marks each act of worship as a covenant renewal.

milgrom.source: Jacob Milgrom, Leviticus 1–6 / 17–22 / 23–27, Anchor Bible (1991–2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Milgrom: The minḥāh in Leviticus represents the agricultural tribute a vassal owes a king. Israel as YHWH's tenant presents a portion of the land's produce — recognition that the land belongs to God, not Israel.

Milgrom: The priestly portion (the non-burned remainder) is called "most holy" — the same designation as the Ark and the inner sanctuary. This elevates the priests' daily food to the level of the sacred; they eat from the altar's excess.

hist.context: The grain offering accompanies the animal offerings as a gift of agricultural produce — the fruit of human labour rather than animal life. It contains no leaven (associated with corruption) and no honey (associated with Canaanite fermented offerings), but always includes salt (the covenant seasoning, v.13) and frankincense. The offering can be raw flour, baked in an oven, on a griddle, or in a pan — five forms in total — reflecting the diversity of Israelite food preparation. The priest burns a representative handful (the ʾazkārāh) and keeps the rest for the priestly household.

heb.paragraph: The first and best of the harvest, presented before the rest is used. Acknowledges that all produce comes from God and belongs to him first.
The representative portion burned on the altar. From zākar, "to remember" — the portion that causes God to "remember" the worshipper favourably. The rest goes to the priests.

refs.note: "Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep." Paul applies the bikkûrîm theology directly to resurrection — Christ is the firstfruits who consecrates the whole harvest of resurrection.
"If the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, so is the whole lump." The firstfruits principle: the first portion consecrates the rest.

notes.note: MacArthur: The firstfruits offering is an act of faith — presenting the grain before the harvest is complete, trusting God to provide the rest. It is the agricultural equivalent of tithing: relinquishing first to acknowledge God's prior claim on all.

MacArthur: "No grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven." Leaven in Scripture consistently symbolises corruption and sin. The exclusion of leaven and honey (which ferments) from the altar reflects the absolute purity required in approaching God. What is offered must be untainted by the processes of decay.

MacArthur: "You shall season all your grain offerings with salt." Salt preserves and purifies — it is the opposite of leaven. The "salt of the covenant" links every offering to the permanence of God's promises. Salt does not decay; neither does God's word. Jesus calls his followers "the salt of the earth" (Matt 5:13), drawing directly on this covenantal image.

MacArthur: The firstfruits offering of crushed new grain represents the earliest and best of the harvest given to God before the worshipper takes any for himself. The principle of firstfruits — God receives the first portion, not the leftovers — runs through both testaments. Paul calls Christ "the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" (1 Cor 15:20).

Calvin: The firstfruits typify Christ, who is himself the first and best offering — the truly holy one presented to God on behalf of all. In him the harvest of redemption is consecrated. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: "Crushed" (gereś) grain — the term appears only here and Lev 2:16. It likely refers to grain that has been roughly milled or cracked rather than ground to fine flour, appropriate for the raw firstfruits presentation.

NET Note: The Hebrew vocabulary in this section on grain offering employs distinctive covenantal terminology. The syntactical construction reinforces the formal, legal register appropriate to the priestly and Deuteronomic tradition.

milgrom.source: Jacob Milgrom, Leviticus 1–6 / 17–22 / 23–27, Anchor Bible (1991–2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Milgrom: The roasted grain (qālî) and crushed grain (gereś) represent the grain at its least processed — raw enough to convey the immediacy of the harvest's first yield. The firstfruits offering is temporally urgent: it happens at the threshold of harvest.

Milgrom: The ritual prescriptions for grain offering reflect a coherent priestly symbolic system. The gradations of holiness and offering type form an internally consistent theology of sacred space and divine access.

hist.context: The firstfruits offering (vv.14–16) is distinct from the regular grain offering — it uses roasted grain or crushed new grain, offered with oil and frankincense. The principle is consistent: the first and best belongs to God before Israel eats of the harvest. This connects to the broader firstfruits theology running through the Torah — firstborn animals (Exod 13), firstborn sons (Exod 13:2), first grain (Num 15:20–21). The logic: what is first consecrates the whole.

ppl.name: Aaron↗ People
ppl.text: Aaron, Moses’s brother, is consecrated as Israel’s first high priest in Lev 8–9. He bears the names of the twelve tribes on his breastplate before God and enters the Most Holy Place once a year on Yom Kippur (Lev 16). His failures — the golden calf (Exod 32), challenging Moses’s authority (Num 12), complicity at Meribah (Num 20) — make him the paradigm of the imperfect priest who prefigures a better high priest (Heb 7:26–28).

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Nadab↗ People
ppl.role: Aaron’s eldest son; died offering unauthorized fire
ppl.text: Nadab son of Aaron is ordained alongside his father in Lev 8–9, ascending to the summit of Israel’s priestly hierarchy. In Lev 10:1–2 he and his brother Abihu offer "unauthorized fire" before the Lord — fire that God had not commanded — and are consumed by divine fire. Aaron’s silence at their death (Lev 10:3) is one of the Torah’s most austere moments. They become the paradigm of presumptuous worship throughout the OT.

ppl.name: Abihu↗ People
ppl.role: Aaron’s second son; died alongside Nadab
ppl.text: Abihu son of Aaron is ordained with his father and brothers in Lev 8–9 and dies with Nadab in Lev 10 offering unauthorized fire. The incident immediately follows the inaugural divine fire accepting the tabernacle’s first official sacrifice — making the contrast between authorized and unauthorized fire as stark as possible. His death, with Nadab’s, establishes the seriousness of the priestly vocation: proximity to God is privilege and peril simultaneously.

ppl.name: Eleazar↗ People
ppl.role: Aaron’s son; second high priest of Israel
ppl.text: Eleazar son of Aaron inherits his father’s high-priestly office on Mount Hor (Num 20:28) — the vestment-transfer ceremony that occurs in Aaron’s presence before his death is the Torah’s most solemn succession. Eleazar serves alongside Joshua to administer the land distribution (Num 34:17) and appears throughout the late Numbers narrative as the priestly counterpart to Joshua’s civil authority. His lineage produces Phinehas and eventually Zadok.

ppl.name: Ithamar↗ People
ppl.role: Aaron’s youngest surviving son; coordinates Levitical clans
ppl.text: Ithamar son of Aaron survives the deaths of Nadab and Abihu to become one of Aaron’s two surviving sons alongside Eleazar. He coordinates the Gershonite and Merarite transport duties (Num 4:28, 33) and receives a significant role in the tabernacle administration. In Lev 10:16–20 Moses initially rebukes him for not eating the sin offering, accepting his explanation.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“ ‘If you bring a grain offering baked in an oven, it is to consist of the finest flour: either thick loaves made without yeast and with olive oil mixed in or thin loaves made without yeast and brushed with olive oil.ESV“When you bring a grain offering baked in the oven as an offering, it shall be unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil or unleavened wafers smeared with oil.NIVBring the grain offering made of these things to the Lord; present it to the priest, who shall take it to the altar.ESVAnd you shall bring the grain offering that is made of these things to the LORD, and when it is presented to the priest, he shall bring it to the altar.

src.title: Ugaritic Sacrifice Texts (KTU 1.40)
src.quote: Ritual texts from Ugarit describe burnt, peace, and purification offerings with comparable priestly procedures and gradations.
src.note: Demonstrates that Israel’s sacrificial vocabulary was shared with the wider ancient Near East, while the theological rationale — holiness, atonement, covenant — was distinctively Israelite.

src.title: Hittite Ritual Texts
src.quote: Hittite purification rituals describe graduated offerings for different levels of transgression, paralleling the Levitical sin-offering gradations of Lev 4–5.
src.note: The structural parallels illuminate the Levitical system’s ANE context while highlighting Israel’s distinctive theology of intentionality and priestly atonement.

src.title: Philo of Alexandria, De Specialibus Legibus (1st c. AD)
src.quote: Philo’s allegorical commentary interprets each offering as symbolic of the soul’s approach to God — the burnt offering as total consecration of the rational soul.
src.note: Influential on early Christian interpretation; Origen and Clement drew heavily on Philo’s allegorical reading of the Levitical laws.

rec.text: "Origen’s homilies on Leviticus are among the most extensive patristic readings of the book, interpreting the sacrificial system allegorically as prefiguring Christ’s work."

hebtext.note: Originally any gift or tribute (Gen 4:3; 32:14). In Leviticus it narrows to the cereal offering. Signals the produce of human labour presented as tribute to the divine King.
The best-grade flour — sifted, premium quality. Only the finest tribute is fitting for God.
The representative portion burned on the altar as an ʾazkārāh (memorial/token portion). The rest belongs to Aaron and his sons.
The first and best of the harvest, presented before the rest is used. Acknowledges that all produce comes from God and belongs to him first.
The representative portion burned on the altar. From zākar, "to remember" — the portion that causes God to "remember" the worshipper favourably. The rest goes to the priests.

thread.text: "I am the bread of life." The grain offering's bread symbolism points forward — Christ is the true bread from heaven, the firstfruits of a new humanity.
"Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed. Therefore let us keep the festival — not with the old leaven… but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth."
The grain offering could be offered both independently (as in Lev 2) and as an accompaniment to animal sacrifices (as in Num 15:1-16).

"Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep." Paul applies the bikkûrîm theology directly to resurrection — Christ is the firstfruits who consecrates the whole harvest of resurrection.
"If the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, so is the whole lump." The firstfruits principle: the first portion consecrates the rest.

tx.title: LXX rendering of the divine speech formula
tx.content: MTwayyiqraʾ("and he called") —LXXkai eklēsenclosely follows MT. The LXX Leviticus is generally a close translation with stylistic smoothing.
tx.note: The LXX Leviticus rarely reflects a divergent Hebrew Vorlage; most variants are stylistic. The Qumran Leviticus scrolls (4QLevᵃᵇ) largely confirm the MT tradition.

tx.title: Samaritan Pentateuch variants
tx.content: MTis the primary witness;SP(Samaritan Pentateuch) occasionally harmonises with Exodus parallels and preserves some archaic forms absent from MT.
tx.note: For Leviticus, the SP variants are minor. The most significant differences involve the location of worship — Gerizim vs Jerusalem — which affects passages relating to the chosen place of sacrifice.

debate.title: Source and Date of The Grain Offering

positions.name: Documentary hypothesis (Wellhausen, Noth)
positions.proponents: Assigns The Grain Offering to the Priestly (P) source, dated to the post-exilic period (6th–5th c. BC) on the basis of vocabulary, theology, and redaction-critical analysis.
positions.argument: This view treats the Levitical legislation as crystallised priestly tradition rather than Mosaic prescription.

positions.name: Unified authorship (Milgrom, Wenham, conservative scholarship)
positions.proponents: Argues that the detailed ANE parallels, archaic vocabulary, and internal coherence support a second-millennium date for The Grain Offering.
positions.argument: Milgrom’s detailed comparison with ANE ritual texts demonstrates the antiquity and coherence of the material regardless of the dating question.

themes.note: The Grain Offering: The Firstfruits Belong to God

coaching.tip: The grain offering accompanies the burnt offering—flour, oil, incense (vv. 1-3). No blood, but still sacred. Labor of fields presented to God. Worship includes the work of human hands, not just animal death.

The grain offering is the only sacrifice that requires no animal death. It sanctifies ordinary agricultural labor — the daily work of grinding, kneading, baking. When Paul tells the Corinthians 'whether you eat or drink, do all to the glory of God' (1 Cor 10:31), Leviticus 2 is the backstory: even bread can be an act of worship.

'Do not burn any yeast or honey in a food offering' (v. 11). Yeast ferments, honey spoils—both symbolize corruption. Salt, however, is required (v. 13): preservative of covenant faithfulness. Ingredients carry theology.

The 'memorial portion' (azkarah) burned on the altar is a fraction of the whole — a handful of flour, a pinch of incense. God doesn't consume everything; the rest feeds the priests. This pattern of divine portion plus human provision recurs throughout the sacrificial system and becomes a principle: giving to God doesn't impoverish the giver.

prayer_prompt: As you read about grain offerings made from the firstfruits of the harvest, consider asking God what 'firstfruits' you hold back from Him — the first of your time, energy, or resources that rightfully belong to Him.
